title: Range type: Type and Value variants
analysis:
This task is ALREADY FULLY IMPLEMENTED in the current worktree. Both Range variants exist with comprehensive trait implementations and 52 Range-specific tests (all passing, 289 total in reify-types).

**Type::Range(Box<Type>)** at ty.rs line 49:
- Derives Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash automatically via derive macros
- Display impl: 'Range<{inner}>' at line 644
- Factory method: Type::range(inner) at lines 109-112
- 8 unit tests (lines 522-588): construction, display (int/scalar/real), factory, eq+hash, not_numeric, as_name_none

**Value::Range { lower, upper, lower_inclusive, upper_inclusive }** at value.rs lines 58-63:
- PartialEq: field-by-field comparison (lines 318-321)
- Eq: marker trait (line 328)
- Ord: type_tag=16, compares lower_inclusive -> lower -> upper_inclusive -> upper (lines 432-440)
- Display: interval notation [0..10] or (-inf..inf) (lines 551-563)
- content_hash: tag=17, flags then optional bounds (lines 252-264)
- Factory: Value::range() with normalization — forces inclusive=false when bound is None (lines 91-105)
- 44 unit tests covering display (8), content_hash (6), ord (7), equality (10), normalization (7), cross-variant (3), hash consistency (3)

**Dimension checking**: Type::Range(Box<Type>) structurally guarantees both bounds share the same element type (and thus dimension for Scalar). Runtime dimension checking is the type-checker's responsibility (in reify-compiler, not reify-types).

**Exports**: tests/exports.rs lines 197-214 verify Range is accessible via public API for both Type and Value.

Since everything is fully implemented and tested, the steps below are verification-only — each confirms existing code and tests are correct.

steps.description: Verify Type::Range(Box<Type>) variant exists with derived Eq/Hash, Display as 'Range<{inner}>', and factory method Type::range(). Run existing 8 tests in ty.rs (type_range_construction through type_range_as_name_none). All should pass — no new test code needed.

Type::Range(Box<Type>) already at ty.rs:49 with derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash), Display at line 644, factory at lines 109-112. No code changes needed — mark as done after step-1 verification.

Verify Value::Range struct variant exists with PartialEq (lines 318-321), Eq (line 328), content_hash tag=17 (lines 252-264). Run existing 15 equality+hash tests (value_range_equal_ranges_are_equal through value_range_content_hash_both_none_deterministic). All should pass.
Value::Range { lower, upper, lower_inclusive, upper_inclusive } already at value.rs:58-63. PartialEq, Eq, content_hash all implemented. No code changes needed.

Verify Value::Range Ord (type_tag=16, lines 432-440) and Display (interval notation, lines 551-563). Run existing 15 ord+display tests (value_range_ord_cross_type_after_orientation through value_range_display_real_bounds). All should pass.

Value::Range Ord and Display already fully implemented. Ord compares lower_inclusive -> lower -> upper_inclusive -> upper. Display uses [/( brackets with -inf/inf for unbounded. No code changes needed.

Verify Value::range() factory normalizes inclusivity (forces false when bound is None) and exports.rs covers Range. Run existing 12 normalization tests (value_range_normalize_* through value_range_display_both_none_with_inclusive_true) and exports test. All should pass.
Value::range() factory at value.rs:91-105 normalizes inclusive flags. exports.rs lines 197-214 test public API. No code changes needed — full implementation is complete.

design_decisions.decision: Type::Range wraps a single Box<Type> rather than two separate bound types
design_decisions.rationale: Structurally enforces that both bounds share the same element type (and thus the same dimension for Scalar types). No runtime validation needed in reify-types — the type checker in reify-compiler handles dimension checking at the type level.

design_decisions.decision: Value::range() factory normalizes inclusive flags to false when bound is None
design_decisions.rationale: Infinity is never 'included', so normalizing prevents logically identical ranges (e.g., (-inf..10] vs [-inf..10]) from comparing as different or producing different content hashes. This is critical for the cache-equality invariant.

design_decisions.decision: Ord for Value::Range uses type_tag=16 (after Orientation=15) and compares lower_inclusive before lower bound
design_decisions.rationale: Tag 16 places Range after Orientation in cross-type ordering, consistent with enum declaration order. Within-type comparison puts inclusive/exclusive status first for deterministic total ordering.

design_decisions.decision: content_hash uses tag byte 17 for Range (tag 10 reserved for Satisfaction, 0-16 for other Value variants)
design_decisions.rationale: Avoids collision with all existing Value and Satisfaction hash tags. The tag-then-flags-then-optional-bounds structure ensures different Range configurations produce distinct hashes.
Standard interval notation is universally understood: [ for inclusive, ( for exclusive, -inf/inf for unbounded. Uses '..' as Rust-idiomatic range separator.

reuse.what: Derive macros on Type enum for automatic Eq/Hash
reuse.how: Type derives Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash — Range(Box<Type>) gets all traits for free via the derive, no manual implementation needed.

reuse.what: ContentHash::of and ContentHash::combine for tagged hashing
reuse.how: Range content_hash follows the same tag+combine pattern used by all other Value variants (List uses tag 7, Tensor uses tag 14, etc.).

reuse.what: f64 to_bits() pattern for total ordering in Value
reuse.how: Range bounds containing Real/Scalar values inherit the to_bits() total ordering through recursive Value comparison — no special f64 handling needed in Range-specific code.

reuse.what: Box<Type> / Option<Box<Value>> boxing pattern for recursive types
reuse.where: Existing compound types: List(Box<Type>), Option(Option<Box<Value>>), Complex(Box<Type>)
reuse.how: Range follows identical boxing: Type::Range(Box<Type>) and Value::Range { lower: Option<Box<Value>>, upper: Option<Box<Value>> }.
Type::range() and Value::range() follow the same convenience-constructor convention. Value::range() adds normalization logic specific to Range semantics.
